With sorrow, and yet also with laughter, he thought of that time. At that time, he recalled, he had boasted three things to Kamala, had mastered three noble and insurmountable arts: Fasting - Waiting - Thinking. This had been his possession, his might and strength, his steady staff, in the busy, toilsome years of his youth he had learned these three arts, nothing else. And now they had left him, none of them was his anymore, not fasting, not waiting, not thinking. He had given her up for what was most miserable, for what was most perishable, for sensual pleasure, for a good life, for riches! It had indeed been strange for him. And now, it seemed, he really had become a childish man.

comment: The first translation is too literal and doesn't quite show understanding of the original ("had become a childish man" - that is not what the original intended to say, the second translation gets this better). The second translation uses better words overall and also better expressions - "His life had indeed been bizarre" is a more accurate and also much better-sounding sentence than "It had indeed been strange for him." The second translation features a better mastery of language and a better representation of the original.

translation_a: The day returned, and it was clearly observed that, under the influence of an undercurrent, the ice bank was drifting rapidly towards the north. This floating mass carried the Forward, nailed to the middle of the ice-field, the limit of which could not be seen; in anticipation of a catastrophe, in case the brig should be thrown on a slope or crushed by the pressure of the ice, Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions, the camping effects, the clothes and blankets of the crew ; Following the example of what Captain MacClure did in a similar circumstance, he had the vessel surrounded by a belt of hammocks inflated with air so as to protect it against serious damage; soon the ice, accumulating under the influence of a temperature of seven degrees (-14脗掳 centigrade); the ship was surrounded by a wall from which its mast alone emerged.

comment: This was a difficult decision. For some reason, I am not a big fan of either translation. Both include great as well as poor phrasing. Ultimately, the reason why I chose the second translation is because the first one contains an error. In French, "[...] Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantit脙漏 de provisions [...]" means that Hatteras HAD SOMEONE do something. He didn't do this thing himself. The translation I selected uses an avoidance strategy: "[...] a large quantity of provisions [...] were brought on deck" where the use of the passive erases agentivity (the person or thing performing the action of the verb). That works, because even though it is not a very accurate translation, it does not contradict the original meaning. The translation I did NOT select, however, did go into this contradiction with: "[...] Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions [...]". translation_a: However, on the 19th Simpson succeeded in seizing one of them four hundred yards from the ship; he had taken the precaution of plugging his hole of refuge, so that the animal was at the mercy of the hunters. He struggled for a long time, and after having suffered several shots, he was finally knocked out. The doctor, wanting to keep his head for his collection of natural history, and his skin for future needs, had both prepared quickly and inexpensively. The adjective "levanti" in French is EXTREMELY rare. Actually I had to look it up myself as I had never seen it before. It seems to be an archaic form for "Turkish soldier" that was only used by Voltaire (the author of this text). In the translation that I selected, the translator chose to sort of explain what "levanti" is by translating "le levanti patron" directly into "the Turkish captain". I think this is a good choice which helps the modern reader understand what's going on without having to look things up. Even if one thinks that keeping the archaic form is best, I highly doubt it would be "levanti boss" - there should at least be a capital L (Levanti boss/captain).

translation_a: As a soccer player Volodya represented a complete opposite to Getzke. Volodya was a professional sportsman芒聙聯the other was a professional player. Important to Volodya was the general course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome芒聙聯Getzke strove only to show his own skill. He was an old, experienced player, not intending to maintain the honor of the team: he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sort of sports organization because he had compromised himself by moves from club to club for money. They forbade him to participate in matches for the play-off of a championship. They invited him only for friendly games, demonstration matches and trips to other countries. Skill united in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He was suspicious of the players芒聙聯both those with whom he played and the opponents. He knew that he would drive in the balls on any team. The rest was not important to him. He was a hack.
translation_b: As a football player, Volodya was the exact opposite of Getska. Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player. Volodya was interested in the overall course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome - Getsuke sought only to show his art. He was an old, experienced player who did not intend to uphold the honor of the team; he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sports organization, because he compromised himself by moving from club to club for money. He was banned from participating in championship matches. He was invited only to a friendly game, exhibition matches and trips to other countries. Art was combined in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents. He knew that he would score goals for any team. The rest didn't matter to him. He was a hack.
comment: There is no doubt that the first translation is better than the second one for the following reasons: 1) There is a mistake in spelling the name of the character in the second translation. It should be "Getzke" while the author of the second translation writes "Getska" or even "Getsuke". 2) According to the source text a story is told about two characters. The second sentence of the passage should give information about two different people while there is only one person mentioned in the second sentence of the 2nd translation: "Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player" 3) The author of the second translation uses a word-by-word translation of the sentence "He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents" which cannot be used here

translation_a: Since he often jumped from one thing to another and forgot what he had begun to say, the prince kept still so as to let him speak everything out. But all the same it was extremely unclear whether the letters had gone through him or through Vera. If he himself insisted that 芒聙聹to Rogozhin was all the same as to Nastasya Filippovna,芒聙?it meant that most likely they had not gone through him, if there were any letters at all. And how it happened that the letter now ended up with him, remained decidedly unexplained; most likely of all would be to suppose that he had somehow stolen it from Vera 芒聙?carried it off on the sly and taken it with some sort of intention to Lizaveta Prokofyevna. So the prince finally figured it out and understood it.
translation_b: Since he often strayed from one to the other and forgot what he began to talk about, the prince fell silent to let him speak. But all the same, it was extremely unclear: did the letters go through him or through Vera? If he himself assured that "to Rogozhin is the same as to Nastasya Filippovna," then it means, rather, that they did not go through him, if only there were letters. The incident, how the letter now came to him, remained decidedly unexplained; most likely it was necessary to assume that he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it with some intention to Lizaveta Prokofievna. So the prince thought and understood at last.
comment: The first translation is a better one for various reasons: Grammar: according to the source text Past perfect should be used to identify a series of actions that happened before other actions in the past. Past Perfect is used in the first translation Vocabulary: "And how it happened that the letter now ended up with him" in the first translation is more accurate and conveys the meaning better than "The incident, how the letter now came to him" in the second translation. The same thing with "carried it off on the sly" (1st translation) over "quietly stole it". Moreover, there is a tautology in the second translation: "he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it". The last sentence in the second translation isn't accurate and doesn't sum up the idea presented in the passage, which makes the sentence in the first translation better.

translation_a: The Christian teaching given to me in childhood disappeared in me as in others, only with the difference that as I began to read a great deal and to think very early on, so my rejection of Christian teaching became a conscious one very early on. From the age of sixteen I stopped saying my prayers and of my own volition stopped going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in all that had been given to me since childhood, but I did believe in something. What I did believe in I couldn芒聙聶t possibly have said. I did believe in God or rather did not deny God, but I couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god; I didn芒聙聶t deny Christ and his teaching, but I also couldn芒聙聶t have said where his teaching lay.
translation_b: The doctrine communicated to me from childhood disappeared in me just as it did in others, with the only difference being that since I began to read and think a lot very early, my renunciation of the doctrine became conscious very early. From the age of sixteen, I stopped standing up for prayer and stopped, on my own impulse, going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in what I was told from childhood, but I believed in something. What I believed in, I could never say. I also believed in God, or rather I did not deny God, but which God I could not say; I did not deny Christ and his teaching, but what his teaching was, I could not say either.
comment: I consider the first translation to be more accurate when it comes to certain details: 1) The source text makes it clear that the narrator tells about Christian teaching, that is why I don't see any reason to call it just "doctrine" as in the second translation 2) The phrase "of my own volition" in the first translation conveys the meaning of the source sentence much better than "on my own impulse" in the second translation 3) According to the source text the narrator "couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god" he believed, that is why the translation "which God I could not say" in the second translation is inappropriate 4) If we consider the context of the source text I believe it's better to use Past Perfect in the sentences that refer to the prior actions in the past. Past Perfect is used in the first translation

translation_a: This unexpected blow nearly killed my father. He lost his usual firmness, and his grief (usually mute) poured out in bitter complaints. "How!" he repeated, losing his temper. - 芒聙聹My son participated in the plans of Pugachev! Good God, what have I come to! The empress saves him from execution! Does that make it easier for me? The execution is not terrible: my ancestor died at the place of execution, defending what he considered the shrine of his conscience; my father suffered along with Volynsky and Khrushchev. But the nobleman should change his oath, unite with robbers, murderers, runaway lackeys!.. Shame and disgrace to our family! human opinion. My father was inconsolable.
translation_b: This unexpected blow very nearly killed my father. He lost his habitual self-control, and his grief, usually silent, found expression in bitter complaints. 芒聙聹What!芒聙?he repeated, beside himself. 芒聙聹My son is an accomplice of Pugachov芒聙聶s! Merciful heavens, what have I lived to see! The Empress reprieves him! Does that make it any better for me? It芒聙聶s not the death penalty that is terrible. My great-grandfather died on the scaffold for what was to him a matter of conscience; my father suffered, together with Volynsky and Khrushchov.a But for a gentleman to betray his oath of allegiance and join brigands, murderers and runaway serfs! Shame and disgrace to our name!芒聙?Terrified by his despair, my mother did not dare to weep in his presence and tried to cheer him by talking of the uncertainty of rumor and the small faith to be attached to people芒聙聶s opinions. My father was inconsolable.
comment: The second translation provides a more accurate translation of the phrases from the source text that cannot be translated word by word. The author of the second translation has managed to perfectly convey the meaning of the sentences "But for a gentleman to betray his oath of allegiance and join brigands, murderers and runaway serfs" (which is translated in a wrong way in the first translation and thus failing to convey the meaning of the Russian sentence) and "Terrified by his despair, my mother did not dare to weep in his presence and tried to cheer him by talking of the uncertainty of rumor and the small faith to be attached to people芒聙聶s opinions" (which is missing in the first translation).

translation_a: He did not finish; Ferdyshchenko quickly put a chair behind him, and the general, somewhat weak on his feet that afternoon, flopped down, or rather, fell on the chair, but this, however, did not embarrass him. He sat down directly opposite Nastasya Filippovna and with a pleasant grin, slowly and effectively, raised her fingers to his lips. In general, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His outward appearance, besides some slovenliness, was still quite decent, which he himself knew very well. He had happened before to be in very good society, from which he was finally excluded only two or three years ago. From the same period, he indulged too much without restraint in some of his weaknesses; but the dexterous and pleasant manner remained in him hitherto. Nastasya Filippovna seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, whom, of course, she knew from hearsay.
translation_b: He did not finish. Ferdyshchenko quickly offered him a chair from behind, and the general, somewhat weak in the legs after dinner, simply flopped or, better to say, collapsed into it; however, that did not embarrass him. He sat directly facing Nastasya Filippovna and, with a pleasant little grimace, slowly and dramatically brought her fingers to his lips. On the whole, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His appearance, apart from a certain slovenliness, was still quite decent, as he knew very well himself. In the past he had occasionally been received in very good society, from which he had been definitively excluded only two or three years ago. It was then that he gave himself over all too unrestrainedly to some of his weaknesses; but he still retained his adroit and pleasant manner. Nastasya Filippovna, it seemed, was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, of whom she knew, of course, by hearsay.
comment: Both translations are quite good, but I prefer the second translation to the first one. The vocabulary in the second translation is more accurate: "weak in the legs" over "weak on his feet", "brought her fingers to his lips" over "raised her fingers to his lips", "was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich" over "seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance". Such small details lead to the understanding of the whole text.
